content.text: Stakeholder mapping for a GenAI programme is not a one-time discovery exercise — it is a living model that determines whether the programme survives from ideation to production. GenAI use cases affect more stakeholders than conventional software: they change how knowledge workers do their jobs, raise data privacy concerns for legal teams, create regulatory exposure for compliance, and threaten existing processes that operational leaders have spent years optimising. A programme that fails to identify and actively manage these stakeholder dynamics will encounter unexpected blocks at funding gates, change resistance during piloting, and compliance vetoes at deployment. The power/interest grid is the foundation of the engagement plan.

attrs.alt: Power/Interest grid for GenAI programme stakeholders: Manage Closely, Keep Satisfied, Keep Informed, Monitor

content.text: The power/interest grid plots every stakeholder on two axes: their power to influence the programme outcome (from blocking funding to enabling rapid deployment) and their interest in the programme (from no impact on their work to fundamental change in how they operate). The four quadrants produce four distinct engagement strategies.

These stakeholders can make or break the programme and are directly affected by it. They require the most intensive engagement: regular one-to-ones, involvement in key design decisions, explicit endorsement sought before funding gates and deployment decisions.

— Chief Claims Officer, Chief Operations Officer, Head of the relevant business unit, Data/AI CoE lead, Chief Financial Officer (if cost savings are the primary value driver). These are the people whose sign-off appears on the funding gate, whose team will use the AI, and whose KPIs will be measured against.
— Weekly or bi-weekly during discovery and build; milestone-based during piloting and deployment.

— A stakeholder in this quadrant who shifts from 'supportive' to 'sceptic' mid-programme is the highest-priority remediation. Their combination of power and interest means they can redirect, delay, or kill the programme faster than any other stakeholder group.

These stakeholders have significant power to block or enable the programme but are not directly affected by its day-to-day outcomes. They care about risk, compliance, and organisational stability rather than the AI's specific functionality.

— Legal Counsel, CISO/DPO, Chief Risk Officer, Internal Audit, Regulator liaison. Legal can block deployment if data processing agreements are not in place. The CISO can block gateway deployment if security reviews are not completed. Internal Audit can raise concerns that require remediation before go-live.

— Involve early in feasibility (not after the business case is set). Proactively share the data governance plan, the AI gateway security architecture, and the compliance review schedule. Surprises for this quadrant are expensive: a legal concern raised at the Gate 4 review that should have been identified in discovery adds months to the timeline.
— Treating 'low interest' as 'can be ignored until needed'. Low interest means they do not think about the programme day-to-day — it does not mean their concerns are minor when they do engage.

These stakeholders are most directly affected by the AI but have limited ability to influence the programme outcome through formal authority. They include the people who will use the AI daily and whose job content will change.

— Frontline staff (adjusters, advisors, analysts), team leads, HR (for workforce planning implications). These are the people whose adoption behaviour determines whether the AI delivers its projected value.
Why this quadrant is often mismanaged

— 'Low power' is mistaken for 'low importance'. Frontline staff cannot block funding, but they can doom a deployment through low adoption, workarounds, and vocal negative feedback that reaches the 'Manage Closely' quadrant. A pilot programme where adjusters refuse to use the AI tool is a programme that will be killed by the operations director they report to.

— Include in user research during discovery. Run targeted pilot groups, not generic rollouts. Build feedback channels into the product so concerns reach the delivery team rather than the operations lead. Communicate clearly and early about what changes and what stays the same.

These stakeholders have low influence and low direct impact. Monitor for stance changes — a vendor who becomes a strategic dependency, or a peer team that becomes a competitor for the same infrastructure budget, can shift quadrants.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal, Optional

Stance = Literal["champion", "supportive", "neutral", "sceptic", "blocker"]
Quadrant = Literal["manage_closely", "keep_satisfied", "keep_informed", "monitor"]

@dataclass
class Stakeholder:
    name: str
    role: str
    power: float        # 1–5: ability to influence programme outcome
    interest: float     # 1–5: degree to which AI affects their work
    stance: Stance
    concerns: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    motivations: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    engagement_owner: str = ""  # who on the AI team manages this relationship

    @property
    def quadrant(self) -> Quadrant:
        high_power   = self.power    >= 3.0
        high_interest = self.interest >= 3.0
        if high_power and high_interest:   return "manage_closely"
        if high_power and not high_interest: return "keep_satisfied"
        if not high_power and high_interest: return "keep_informed"
        return "monitor"

    @property
    def priority_score(self) -> float:
        # Blockers in high-power positions get highest priority
        stance_weight = {"blocker": 2.0, "sceptic": 1.5, "neutral": 1.0,
                         "supportive": 0.8, "champion": 0.5}
        return self.power * self.interest * stance_weight[self.stance]

    def engagement_strategy(self) -> str:
        q = self.quadrant
        if q == "manage_closely":
            return "Weekly briefings; involve in key decisions; seek explicit endorsement before major milestones"
        if q == "keep_satisfied":
            return "Monthly updates; address concerns proactively; involve in governance reviews"
        if q == "keep_informed":
            return "Regular newsletters / town halls; feedback channels for concerns; clear change communication"
        return "Periodic updates; monitor for stance changes; escalate if power or interest increases"


@dataclass
class StakeholderMap:
    use_case: str
    stakeholders: list[Stakeholder] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add(self, s: Stakeholder) -> "StakeholderMap":
        self.stakeholders.append(s)
        return self

    def blockers_by_priority(self) -> list[Stakeholder]:
        blockers = [s for s in self.stakeholders if s.stance in ("blocker", "sceptic")]
        return sorted(blockers, key=lambda s: s.priority_score, reverse=True)

    def champions(self) -> list[Stakeholder]:
        return [s for s in self.stakeholders if s.stance == "champion"]

    def coverage_gaps(self) -> list[str]:
        """Identify quadrants with no champions."""
        champion_quadrants = {s.quadrant for s in self.stakeholders if s.stance == "champion"}
        required = {"manage_closely", "keep_satisfied"}
        missing = required - champion_quadrants
        return [f"No champion in '{q}' quadrant — high risk" for q in missing]

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"=== Stakeholder Map: {self.use_case} ===",
                 f"Total stakeholders: {len(self.stakeholders)}"]
        for q in ("manage_closely", "keep_satisfied", "keep_informed", "monitor"):
            group = [s for s in self.stakeholders if s.quadrant == q]
            lines.append(f"  {q}: {[s.name for s in group]}")
        lines.append(f"Champions: {[s.name for s in self.champions()]}")
        lines.append("Top blockers/sceptics by priority:")
        for s in self.blockers_by_priority()[:3]:
            lines.append(f"  {s.name} ({s.role}) — score={s.priority_score:.1f}")
            if s.concerns:
                lines.append(f"    Concerns: {s.concerns[0]}")
        gaps = self.coverage_gaps()
        if gaps:
            lines.append("Coverage gaps: " + "; ".join(gaps))
        return "\n".join(lines)


# FNOL classification stakeholder map
fnol_map = StakeholderMap("FNOL Auto-Classification")
fnol_map.add(Stakeholder(
    "Sarah Chen", "Chief Claims Officer",
    power=4.5, interest=4.5, stance="champion",
    motivations=["Reduce handling time by 30%", "Improve adjuster capacity"],
    engagement_owner="Programme Sponsor",
))
fnol_map.add(Stakeholder(
    "Mark Peters", "CFO",
    power=4.0, interest=3.5, stance="supportive",
    motivations=["Cost reduction target", "ROI demonstration"],
    concerns=["Build vs buy cost comparison not complete"],
    engagement_owner="Programme Sponsor",
))
fnol_map.add(Stakeholder(
    "Aisha Okonkwo", "Legal Counsel",
    power=4.5, interest=2.0, stance="sceptic",
    concerns=["GDPR consent for AI use of claimant text", "Audit trail for automated classifications"],
    engagement_owner="AI Lead",
))
fnol_map.add(Stakeholder(
    "James Rivera", "Head of Claims Operations",
    power=3.0, interest=4.5, stance="sceptic",
    concerns=["Adjusters will lose skills if AI does classification", "Accuracy concerns on unusual claim types"],
    motivations=["Reduce admin burden on team"],
    engagement_owner="Change Lead",
))
fnol_map.add(Stakeholder(
    "Claims Adjusters (×42)", "Frontline Staff",
    power=2.0, interest=5.0, stance="neutral",
    concerns=["Job security", "Accuracy of classification on edge cases"],
    motivations=["Less repetitive admin work"],
    engagement_owner="Change Lead",
))

print(fnol_map.summary())

The stance dimension is as important as the power/interest quadrant. A champion in the 'Keep Informed' quadrant can mobilise peer pressure that shifts neutral colleagues. A blocker in the 'Manage Closely' quadrant requires a structured remediation plan before the programme can proceed. Each stance requires a different engagement approach:
— Actively advocate for the programme in forums the AI team does not attend. Provide access to data, teams, and infrastructure. Absorb organisational resistance on the programme's behalf.

— Champions need air cover and early wins to sustain their advocacy. Deliver visible value quickly in their domain. Keep them informed of risks before those risks become public so they are not surprised in executive forums. Ask explicitly what they need from the programme to maintain their advocacy.

— Champions who become over-exposed (programme takes longer than promised, early results disappointing) can shift to neutral or sceptic to protect their own credibility. Do not over-promise to champions.

— Raise substantive objections in steering committees. Ask hard questions about accuracy, job impact, and data privacy that the programme team has not publicly addressed. Their scepticism is usually rational, not political.

— Engage directly with the substance of their concerns rather than around them. Invite sceptics into the pilot design so their specific concerns can be tested empirically. A sceptic who is proven wrong by evidence often becomes the programme's most credible internal advocate — they have no interest in continuing to object once their concern is resolved.
— Excluding sceptics from forums to avoid discomfort. A sceptic who is not engaged raises concerns in forums the AI team is not present in, where those concerns cannot be addressed.
— Actively work against the programme: escalating risks to boards, withholding access to data or systems, mobilising other stakeholders against the programme.

— Diagnose whether the blocking behaviour is substantive (a real concern that has not been addressed) or political (a threat to existing power structures, budget, or headcount). Substantive blockers can often be converted to sceptics then to neutral by addressing their concern directly. Political blockers require executive sponsor intervention — the blocker's concerns need to be addressed at the power level at which they operate.

— If a blocker in the 'Manage Closely' quadrant cannot be moved through direct engagement within two weeks, escalate to the executive sponsor. Leaving a high-power blocker unaddressed for more than a month is a programme risk.

The stakeholder map produces an engagement plan that is a formal deliverable of the discovery phase. It documents who is responsible for each relationship, the engagement cadence, and the open concerns that must be resolved:
Share updated delivery plan by Friday
Schedule DPA review session this week
Design adjuster involvement in pilot selection
Share gateway architecture diagram for review
Run Q&A session before pilot launch

GenAI programmes require governance stakeholders to be engaged differently from conventional software projects because the governance surface is larger: data processing agreements, AI ethics reviews, model risk assessments, and regulatory notifications are all new to most enterprise governance teams.

— Under GDPR, the DPO must be consulted when processing activities are likely to result in high risk to individuals. AI-driven decision-making on insurance claims qualifies. The DPO is not a rubber stamp — they have an independent function and can formally object to processing activities. Engage before the business case is finalised.

— Banks and insurers with a Model Risk Management (MRM) function under SR 11-7 (US) or equivalent require that AI models used in decision-making be validated by the MRM team before deployment. The MRM validation adds 4–12 weeks to the delivery timeline. Identify MRM requirements at the feasibility gate, not after build.

— In EU jurisdictions and some UK contexts, employee representative bodies must be consulted before implementing systems that monitor or affect employee work. AI tools that classify or route adjuster work may trigger consultation requirements under the Information and Consultation of Employees Regulations. HR and Legal must confirm consultation requirements before build begins.

— For use cases that affect customer outcomes (claims decisions, pricing, underwriting), the Board Risk Committee may require a periodic AI risk report. Agree the reporting format with the Chief Risk Officer before go-live — retrofitting board reporting onto a deployed AI system is more complex than designing it in from the start.
Stance Dynamics: Tracking Changes Over Time

Stakeholder stances are not fixed. A champion whose team is passed over for the pilot can shift to neutral. A sceptic whose specific concern is addressed by evidence can shift to supportive. The stakeholder map must be reviewed at each project milestone and updated when stances change:
Immediate briefing of all 'Manage Closely' stakeholders before results become public; root cause analysis in hand
Neutral → Supportive (urgency increases); Monitor → Keep Informed
Update business case to include competitive pressure; refresh executive sponsor's talking points
Regulatory guidance published on AI in the domain
Engage legal within 1 week; commission compliance gap analysis
Workforce restructuring announced in same period
Decouple AI narrative from headcount; HR lead takes ownership of communication
Executive sponsor departs or changes role
Identify replacement sponsor within 2 weeks; programme is at risk until confirmed
Amplify — invite to steering committee demos; use as peer references for sceptic colleagues

Assign a reviewer to the stakeholder map at each milestone gate. In the 'Manage Closely' quadrant, a stance change from supportive to sceptic that is not caught within two weeks of the trigger event typically costs 4–8 weeks of programme delay to remediate. Early detection is significantly cheaper than late remediation.
GenAI programmes encounter resistance patterns that are distinct from conventional software delivery. Understanding these patterns allows the engagement plan to address them before they escalate:
'The AI will take my job' narrative

— This is the most common resistance driver among frontline staff and their union representatives. It is rarely addressed by denying the job impact; it is addressed by being explicit about what will change (the classification step, the first-draft generation) and what will not (the expert judgement, the customer relationship, the decision accountability). Use case framing matters: 'AI that handles admin so adjusters can focus on customer empathy' is received differently from 'AI that automates adjuster work'.
'We tried this before and it didn't work' scepticism

— Many enterprises have run previous ML or RPA initiatives that were declared successes and quietly wound down. Stakeholders who lived through those initiatives carry institutional memory of unmet promises. Address this by being precise about what is different (model capability, evaluation methodology, integration approach) and what is the same (it still requires good data, clear objectives, and user adoption). Avoid generic AI hype language with this audience.
'Who is accountable when the AI is wrong?' concern

— This is a legitimate governance concern, not political resistance. GenAI models can produce confident incorrect outputs; in regulated industries, incorrect outputs on claims decisions, financial advice, or medical triage carry real liability. The answer is not 'the model is accurate' (a claim that will be tested) but 'here is the accountability structure: the AI recommends, the adjuster reviews, the adjuster is accountable for the decision'. Embed the accountability model into the product design and communicate it to legal and compliance before deployment.
'This is a data privacy issue' veto from legal

— A legal veto at Gate 4 that cites data privacy is almost always preventable if legal is engaged at feasibility. The veto is not about privacy being impossible to address — it is about the build team not having engaged legal early enough for privacy-by-design to be incorporated. The engagement plan must have legal as an active participant from discovery, not a reviewer at the end.
'The model accuracy isn't good enough' from operations

— Operational leaders who live with accuracy targets (SLAs, error rates, regulatory quality standards) have legitimate concerns about AI accuracy. Address this by agreeing the evaluation criteria and accuracy thresholds before build, not after. If the operations director defines 'good enough' as 95% classification accuracy and the model achieves 87%, that is not a communications problem — it is a scope problem that requires either improving the model, narrowing the use case, or changing the deployment model (higher confidence threshold with human fallback).

The power/interest grid classifies stakeholders into four quadrants: Manage Closely (high power, high interest — weekly engagement, involved in key decisions), Keep Satisfied (high power, low interest — proactive risk communication, no surprises), Keep Informed (low power, high interest — adoption-critical; often underserved), and Monitor (low power, low interest — watch for quadrant shifts).

Stance is as important as position. A high-power sceptic requires direct substantive engagement with their concerns. A high-power blocker requires executive sponsor intervention. Excluding sceptics from forums does not resolve their concerns — it relocates them to forums the AI team cannot address.

The stakeholder engagement plan is a discovery deliverable, not a project management formality. It documents the engagement owner, cadence, open concerns, and next actions for every stakeholder in the top two quadrants.

Governance stakeholders (DPO, MRM function, Works Council, Board Risk Committee) have longer lead times than operational stakeholders. Engage them at feasibility, not at deployment. Identify which regulatory consultation or validation obligations apply before the business case is funded.

Stances shift at milestone events. Review the stakeholder map at each gate and after high-impact events (pilot results, regulatory guidance, leadership changes). A stance shift from supportive to sceptic in the 'Manage Closely' quadrant undetected for two weeks typically costs 4–8 weeks of programme delay.

GenAI resistance patterns are predictable: job security fears from frontline staff, institutional scepticism from previous failed initiatives, accountability concerns from governance teams, and privacy vetoes from legal. Each pattern has a known mitigation — address them by design, not by reaction.
